strengths: Employment growth aided by favorable 25-34 year-old population growth trends
Positive in-migration partially driven by corporate relocations, particularly Midtown and CBD
Low personal income tax rates boost the market's desirability
weaknesses: Local government's openness to rezoning creates stroke-of-the-pen risk to supply barriers
Lack of strong local universities weighs on long-term income growth (#39 of 50 income growth forecast)
Low supply barriers encourage development; Midtown submarket particularly active

rankings.cat: U.S. Headquarters — SMBC lands in Uptown (~2,000 jobs by 2032)

inmig.o: Corporate relocations — SMBC 2nd U.S. HQ, Uptown
inmig.note: >$50M investment by fall 2027; ~2,000 jobs planned by 2032
BofA + Truist HQ, Wells Fargo East Coast hub; Financial Activities 9% of employment
Atrium Health $168M University City patient tower (online late 2031); +16,800 healthcare jobs TTM

realpage.v: Effective rents bent, not broke: -2.1% YoY to $1,516 (Berkadia 1Q26) vs Austin's -7.8% at trough. Concessions average 3.8% across our 698-property universe.
Deliveries 16,953 (2024) → 14,622 (2025) → 13,793 (2026F) before the cliff: 7,769 (2027F), 5,159 (2028F). The digestion window runs through 2026.
11,553 units absorbed trailing-four-quarters (Berkadia); 13,218 on CoStar's count. Third straight year of five-digit absorption.
CoStar base case: metro vacancy tops at 13.4% in 27Q1, rent growth positive by late 2027. Northmarq is earlier: stabilized vacancy 8.5% by YE26, rents +0.5% in 2026.
+21,100 households (+1.8%) in the trailing twelve months against 1.18M total — supply is the problem, demand is not.
~8,000 units broke ground in 2025 vs 11,700 in 2024 (Northmarq); 26Q1 starts pace annualizes near decade lows. The 2027-28 trough is already locked in.

apartmentTrends.v: 94.2% stabilized occupancy (Oct 2025), up 30 bps YoY, despite 6.6% of stock delivering in 2025 — more than twice the 2.8% national pace (Yardi Matrix).
Upscale Lifestyle rents -1.1% YoY ($1,670); Renter-by-Necessity -0.7% ($1,326). The supply wave is a Class A problem; workforce product is steady.
Wessex Square +10.6% YoY ($1,763), Mt. Holly +7.9% ($1,551), Northwest Charlotte +5.4% ($1,330). Uptown is the most expensive micro at $2,150, down 2.3%.
Of 52 Yardi micro-submarkets, 8 hold >1,000 units underway — Tryon Hills (3,775), North Charlotte (3,639), Uptown (2,147) lead. 32 micros have ≥50 units underway.
26,898 units under construction (Nov 2025) plus ~92,000 planned/prospective. Low barriers mean the next wave can re-load — watch starts, not just deliveries.
$1.6B traded through Nov 2025, flat to 2024; avg $208,936/unit, +16.9% YoY. Loft One35 traded at $315,436/unit — institutional appetite for new product is back.

newmark.v: Asking rents +0.4% QoQ to $1,559 in 1Q26 after a -2% second half of 2025 — first quarterly increase of the cycle (Northmarq).
8.7% stabilized-basis vacancy, up 30 bps QoQ and 70 bps YoY — three consecutive quarters of softening. Forecast: stabilizes near 8.5% by year-end.
12,359 units forecast for 2026 delivery vs ~16,100 in 2025. Q1 completions of ~3,550 ran 24% below prior year.
SMBC selected Charlotte for its second U.S. headquarters (Uptown): >$50M investment by fall 2027, ~2,000 jobs by 2032. 2026 job growth forecast +1.4% (+20,000).
Q1 volume -56% YoY, but 75% of YTD sales are post-2020 vintage; median price $255,500/unit, +21% vs 2024. Transactional yields ~4.75%.
South End rents $2,246 — a >$260/mo premium to market — with >2,600 net move-ins TTM and vacancy of just 7.2% despite the metro wave.

UC_DEALS.n: The Oasis at Bailey's Glen Senior Condos
Ford City Motor Lofts Senior Lofts

EXEC_NARRATIVE.lead: Charlotte is a Green Street A- market ranked 22nd of 50, and the rank tells the story: top-5 demand chained to bottom-10 supply barriers. The metro delivered a record 16,953 net units in 2024 and another 14,622 in 2025 — roughly 6.6% of standing stock per year, more than twice the national pace. The correction that bought is shallow but unfinished: vacancy sits at 12.5% (26Q1, all-asset basis), stabilized occupancy at 93.8%, and effective rent growth troughed at -2.4% YoY in 26Q1 — versus Austin's -7.8% at its trough. Concessions across our 698-property universe average 3.8%. Rents bent; they did not break.
EXEC_NARRATIVE.thesis: Demand is the constant: 13,218 units absorbed in the trailing twelve months, a third straight year of five-digit absorption, on +54,000 residents (5th nationally) and #3-of-50 forecast job growth anchored by financial services and the SMBC second-headquarters win. Supply is the variable: under-construction units have fallen 48% from the 33,431-unit peak (23Q2) to 17,493 (26Q1) — about 1.3 years of demand — and 2025 starts of ~8,000 lock in a 2027-28 delivery trough. The wedge is submarket variance. South End absorbed 2,738 units TTM against a pipeline worth three months of that demand; LoSo and West Charlotte show the same shape. The county ring (York, Iredell, Lancaster, Rowan) is already at or near stabilized vacancy with empty pipelines. Huntersville/Cornelius (18.6% vacant, 14.4% of stock underway) and North Charlotte (19.5% of stock underway) are 2027-28 stories — distress-entry candidates, not stabilization plays.
EXEC_NARRATIVE.timing: On CoStar's base case the metro gets worse before better: vacancy peaks at 13.4% in 27Q1 and metro rent growth turns positive in late 2027. The brokers are earlier — Northmarq sees vacancy stabilizing at 8.5% (stabilized basis) and rents +0.5% in 2026, and 1Q26 printed the cycle's first quarterly rent increase. Both curves agree on direction: 2026 deliveries of ~13,800 are the last heavy year, and the submarkets that cleared their pipelines are already recovering. Metro-average underwriting is four to six quarters early; submarket-level underwriting is on time now in South End, LoSo, West Charlotte, and the stabilized county ring.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Supply Re-Load (Stroke-of-the-Pen)
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Green Street ranks Charlotte 43rd of 50 on supply growth and flags the local government's rezoning openness as direct risk to what few barriers exist. ~92,000 units sit in planning/prospective stages (Yardi). If starts re-accelerate above ~2,500/quarter in 2026, the 2027-28 trough thesis breaks. Monitor CoStar quarterly starts.

RISK_FACTORS.label: 2026 Is Still a Heavy Year
RISK_FACTORS.detail: 13,793 net units deliver in 2026 (CoStar base case) and metro vacancy doesn't peak until 27Q1 at 13.4%. Underwriting metro-average rent growth before 2027 is early; the thesis only works at submarket level.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Demand Concentration in Financial Services
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Financial activities plus professional/business services drive the in-migration engine (BofA, Truist, Wells Fargo hub, SMBC incoming). A banking-sector employment shock hits Charlotte harder than a diversified metro. Office-using job growth is the single variable to watch.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Income Growth Ceiling
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Green Street ranks Charlotte 39th of 50 on 5-year income growth, citing weak local universities — the metro imports its talent. Rent-to-income is 21.1% and rising rents lean on continued in-migration rather than wage growth. If migration slows, pricing power goes with it.

RISK_FACTORS.label: Lease-Up Overhang in Specific Submarkets
RISK_FACTORS.detail: Huntersville/Cornelius (18.6% vacant, 14.4% of stock underway) and North Charlotte (19.5% underway) carry 2+ years of supply at current absorption. Uptown posted negative TTM absorption. Basis opportunities will appear here in 2027 — entering early means catching the knife.

EMPLOYERS.n: Novant Health Mint Hill Medical Center

SUB_NARRATIVES.South End: Demand engine: 17,176 units, 16.4% vacancy concentrated in lease-up product, and 2,738 units absorbed TTM against 2,728 delivered (1.00x). Remaining pipeline is 787 units — three months of demand. Metro-high rents ($2,192 CoStar; $2,246 Northmarq, a >$260 premium) with vacancy already past peak (26Q1). CoStar 2026F rent growth +1.5%, best of the urban subs.
SUB_NARRATIVES.LoSo - Lower South End: 5,956 units, 17.0% vacancy, 1.68x absorption/delivery TTM (1,268 absorbed vs 753 delivered). Pipeline 320 units (5.4%). Rents repriced -8.3% from peak to $1,730 — the lease-up discount window. Atrium Health and LendingTree anchor. Northmarq flags LoSo + South End as where transaction activity concentrates.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Uptown Charlotte: Caution flag despite the address: 8,753 units, 13.1% vacancy, negative TTM absorption (-89 units) against 353 delivered, with 701 units (8.0%) still underway. Rents -10.2% from peak, steepest in the metro. Wells Fargo, Truist, Honeywell, and the incoming SMBC second U.S. HQ anchor long-term demand; near-term, the office-conversion question is unresolved. 2026F rent growth -3.2%.
SUB_NARRATIVES.West Charlotte: 21,841 units, 17.2% vacancy, but 1,991 units absorbed TTM against 2,059 delivered (0.97x) — lease-up is clearing. Pipeline down to 1,111 units (5.1%). River District (1,400 acres, NOVEL River District pre-leasing) is the long-term story; Coca-Cola Consolidated and the CLT airport complex anchor jobs. Vacancy peaked 26Q1 on CoStar's curve.
SUB_NARRATIVES.South Charlotte: Largest submarket: 35,877 units at 8.7% vacancy. The flag is demand — only 99 units absorbed TTM against 420 delivered (0.24x), with 2,636 units (7.3%) still underway. Low vacancy reflects little delivering until now, not strength. Carmel/Ballantyne wealth (GS grades Carmel A), Compass Group HQ. Watch absorption before adding exposure.
SUB_NARRATIVES.South Park: 12,615 units, 12.1% vacancy, 0.22x A/D TTM (172 absorbed vs 790 delivered). Pipeline 705 units (5.6%). Maps to Green Street's Midtown geography — the metro's only A+ grade and only High-barrier submarket, $130K median HHI. Soft current absorption against the metro's best structural scarcity. Nucor and Sonic Automotive HQs.
SUB_NARRATIVES.North Charlotte: The supply story: 4,008 units underway on 20,561 — 19.5% of stock, heaviest in the metro. Absorption is real (1,677 TTM, 1.34x A/D) but vacancy doesn't peak until 27Q2 on CoStar's curve. Scout Motors and AvidXchange anchor; Tryon Hills/NoDa micros lead Yardi's UC table. Distress-entry candidate for 2027, not a stabilization play.
SUB_NARRATIVES.East Charlotte: 27,725 units, 11.9% vacancy, 0.67x A/D TTM (698 vs 1,046). Pipeline 1,604 units (5.8%). Contains NoDa and Plaza Midwood micros; workforce-heavy stock at $1,462 average rent. Novant Mint Hill and Harris Teeter HQ anchor. 2026F rent growth -1.5% — middle of the pack, recovery follows the urban core.
SUB_NARRATIVES.University: 25,826 units, 15.1% vacancy, 0.59x A/D TTM (1,147 vs 1,931). Pipeline 1,456 units (5.6%). UNC Charlotte (30K students) and TIAA / Atrium's $168M University City expansion anchor. Northmarq calls out University fundamentals stabilizing as supply moderates. Lease-up overhang clears through 2026.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Huntersville/Cornelius: The metro's deepest lease-up hole: 18.6% vacancy on 9,027 units with another 1,299 (14.4%) underway and 0.39x A/D TTM. Two-plus years of supply at current absorption. Premium suburban product ($1,746) near Lake Norman; Trane Technologies anchors. CoStar shows no stabilization inside the forecast window — distress watch list.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Cabarrus County: 12,106 units, 10.3% vacancy, 0.76x A/D TTM with 1,264 units (10.4%) underway — vacancy rises through 27Q4 on CoStar's curve. Concord's Amazon sort center and Speedway Motorsports anchor. GS grades Concord A- on desirability 82. Patience: the pipeline is still arriving.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Gaston County: 11,522 units, 7.7% vacancy, 1.13x A/D TTM. Pipeline 859 units (7.5%) — manageable against a tight base. $1,419 rents, workforce profile, Yardi's Mt. Holly micro (+7.9% YoY) sits here. Quietly one of the steadier county positions.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Iredell County: 11,320 units, 11.3% vacancy clearing fast — 2.10x A/D TTM (723 absorbed vs 345 delivered), pipeline just 404 units (3.6%). Lowe's HQ (Mooresville) anchors. Vacancy already back near stabilized on CoStar's curve. Lake Norman north-shore growth corridor.
SUB_NARRATIVES.York County: 15,243 units at 7.9% vacancy — peaked 26Q1, effectively stabilized, with zero CoStar-tracked units underway. 1.13x A/D TTM. Fort Mill schools are the suburban demand magnet; Sunbelt Rentals HQ. SC tax differential (3.0% flat income tax by 2027) sharpens the relocation pitch. No supply risk; no distress either.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Lancaster County: 3,667 units, 8.5% vacancy, +4.5% ERG — the metro's strongest current rent growth — with an empty pipeline and 283 units absorbed TTM. Indian Land panhandle is functionally a South Charlotte extension at a lower basis ($1,724). Continental Tire anchors. Small base, top-decile forecast growth (2026F +2.3%).
SUB_NARRATIVES.Rowan County: 2,669 units, 7.7% vacancy, zero pipeline, 334 units absorbed TTM. $1,248 rents — the metro's value end. US Foods distribution anchors. 2026F rent growth +1.8% on scarcity. Too small for scale; fine for one-off bases.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Lincoln County: 1,795 units, 6.5% vacancy, zero meaningful pipeline, negative TTM absorption (-37) on a tiny base. Timken and LeeBoy anchor a manufacturing economy. Tightest occupancy in the metro but no depth — monitor only.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Union County: 5,472 units, 10.4% vacancy, and the metro's best A/D ratio: 6.56x (781 absorbed vs 119 delivered). Pipeline 320 units (5.8%). Monroe/Waxhaw growth with top-rated schools; 2026F +0.9%. Supply-starved suburban demand — the quiet overperformer.
SUB_NARRATIVES.Chester County: 210 tracked units — a placeholder row. 4.7% vacancy, 11 units underway, no properties in our 100+ unit universe (the one 1986-vintage asset is excluded by the 2000+ filter). Carolina Panthers' abandoned Rock Hill project aside, this is exurban spillover to watch, not invest.

DV.props: CoStar property-level data, 26Q1 export (pulled May 2026). Occupancy, vacancy, concession, and rent fields reflect the Q1 2026 snapshot. 100+ unit, built-2000+ universe.
DV.rp: Berkadia 1Q26 market report (RealPage data) + Northmarq 1Q26 annual forecast tables. Forecasts 2026F-2028F.
DV.at: Yardi Matrix Charlotte market analysis, January 2026 (November 2025 data). Lifestyle/RBN segmentation.
DV.ms: Market Stadium zip data, 2026 pull. Note: ten factor columns shipped as percentile scores without actual values in this vintage — affected factors are blanked, weights redistribute.
DV.acs: American Community Survey 2024 + Zillow ZHVI through 2026.
DV.nm: Northmarq Charlotte Multifamily Market Insights, Q1 2026.
DV.gs: Green Street Apartment Market Snapshot — Charlotte, Q1 2026.
DV.sales: CoStar Charlotte sales export, Jan 2020 - May 2026. NC is a disclosure state: 431 of 453 trades have prices.
DV.ts: CoStar market & submarket quarterly series, 16Q1-31Q1; actuals through 26Q1, base-case forecast beyond.
DV.ann: Annual deliveries/absorption derived from the CoStar submarket quarterly series.
DV.score: Composite scoring runs at load with default weights 25/40/35. Zip layer operates on 10 of 16 factors for Charlotte pending Market Stadium actual-value re-pull.
DV.subNarr: Submarket narratives written June 2026 from 26Q1 data; statistical claims are quarter-specific.
